meta.input_text:
Dataset and Sessions  
Source: WAVi Wellness Basic Report generated 9/20/2024 9:30 AM using WAVi Desktop 1.0.0.2; male, DOB 4/8/1997, age 27 for all sessions.  
Session timing and context: Session 1 (Baseline) on 8/5/2024 9:56 AM (P300 Eyes Closed 4 min); Session 2 (Follow-up) on 8/26/2024 11:32 AM; Session 3 (Follow-up) on 9/20/2024 8:49 AM. Flanker and coherence acquisitions are bracketed within these same dates (Flanker Session 1 at 10:01 AM on 8/5/2024; Session 2 at 11:37 AM on 8/26/2024; Session 3 at 8:54 AM on 9/20/2024).  
Self-report scales: HAM-A and PHQ-9 are present but recorded as N/A in all sessions (targets ≤17 and \<5 respectively). No symptom narratives, diagnoses, or treatment records are included.  
Task instructions and hardware details are not described; sleep and time-since-meal are marked N/A, limiting state interpretation.  
Symbol key: low yield indicated by ◘ on the summary page; no other symbols appear on summary metrics for P300 or behavioral tests. Low-yield flag is present for CZ theta/beta in all sessions.  
Key Empirical Findings  
P300 latency (audio, central-parietal aggregate) moved 264 ms → 240 ms → 244 ms (target 246–320 ms). Session 2 achieved the fastest qualifying latency; Session 3 remained faster than Session 1 and just below the lower target bound.  
P300 voltage (audio, central-parietal maximum) rose 17.9 µV → 23.4 µV → 24.2 µV (target 9–22 µV). Sessions 2–3 exceed the upper reference, with CZ the dominant site each time.  
N100 central-frontal average became faster and shallower: \-8.8 µV at 112 ms → \-7.7 µV at 104 ms → \-5.1 µV at 100 ms, yields 37 → 36 → 38\.  
Peak alpha frequency (eyes closed) slowed uniformly across frontal, central-parietal, and occipital regions: 10.0 Hz → 10.0 Hz → 9.5 Hz (target 9.0–11.0 Hz); still within range.  
CZ theta/beta ratio (eyes closed power) decreased from 2.3 (◘) to 1.4 (◘) then 1.5 (◘), moving from the high to mid reference band (1.0–2.5) but consistently flagged low yield.  
Behavioral performance: Physical reaction time 253 ms → 238 ms → 287 ms (target 260–374 ms); Trail Making Test A 39 s → 36 s → 45 s (target 37–63 s); Trail Making Test B 105 s → 57 s → 58 s (target 47–90 s). Session 2 shows the best performance across all three; Session 3 retains the large TMT-B gain but reaction time regresses above Session 1\. (Peer feedback correction: earlier claim that TMT-B was faster than TMT-A was wrong—TMT-B is longer in all sessions, as expected for a more complex task.)  
Performance Assessments  
Physical Reaction Time (ms; target 260–374): 253 (±46) → 238 (±37) → 287 (±50). Session 2 outperforms the lower bound, indicating faster responses; Session 3 slows relative to both prior sessions and exceeds the Session 1 latency by 34 ms.  
Trail Making Test A (s; target 37–63): 39 → 36 → 45\. Session 2 is fastest; Session 3 increases by 9 s over Session 2 yet remains in-range.  
Trail Making Test B (s; target 47–90): 105 → 57 → 58\. Large improvement of 48 s between Sessions 1 and 2, maintained in Session 3 (+1 s). TMT-B remains longer than TMT-A in every session (correcting prior misstatement flagged by peer review).  
Relative task relationships: Session 2 shows consistent improvement across RT, TMT-A, and TMT-B. Session 3 retains improved TMT-B but not RT; TMT-A increases but stays within target.  
Context limits: Sleep, meal status, and medications are N/A; without these, intra-subject variability in RT and TMT cannot be attributed to state or practice effects confidently.  
Variability: The swing in RT from Session 2 to 3 is \~49 ms; TMT-A swing from Session 2 to 3 is 9 s; TMT-B is stable within 1 s between Sessions 2 and 3\. Such differences justify repeat testing under standardized conditions before inferring true change.  
Auditory ERP: P300 and N100  
P300 definitions (Appendix): Delay is the fastest central-parietal latency between 240–499 ms among sites ≥3 µV with yield ≥20; Voltage is the largest central-parietal amplitude 240–499 ms with yield ≥20. Low yield is not indicated for the reported metrics; summary metrics are presented without the low-yield symbol.  
Central-parietal P300 per-site with yields (µV / ms / yield): | Site | Session 1 | Session 2 | Session 3 | |---|---|---|---| | C3 | 12.0 / 300 / 37 | 10.3 / 320 / 35 | 14.0 / 244 / 40 | | CZ | 17.9 / 284 / 40 | 23.4 / 240 / 37 | 24.2 / 244 / 37 | | C4 | 10.4 / 288 / 39 | 16.2 / 308 / 36 | 13.3 / 248 / 40 | | P3 | 12.5 / 296 / 39 | 12.1 / 252 / 33 | 11.7 / 248 / 38 | | PZ | 11.4 / 288 / 40 | 18.2 / 244 / 38 | 12.6 / 244 / 39 | | P4 | 9.7 / 264 / 34 | 13.4 / 248 / 38 | 8.7 / 248 / 40 |  
Summary P300 (central-parietal aggregate):  
Delay: 264 ms → 240 ms → 244 ms (target 246–320). Session 2 is fastest; Session 3 remains faster than Session 1\.  
Voltage: 17.9 µV → 23.4 µV → 24.2 µV (target 9–22). Sessions 2–3 exceed the upper target; Session 1 is within range.  
Test/retest changes (report): latency \-24 ms then \-20 ms (typical variance ±11 ms); voltage \+5 µV then \+6 µV (typical variance ±2 µV), indicating changes beyond usual test-retest thresholds.  
Topographic and temporal observations:  
Session 1 earliest qualifying latency among CP sites is P4 at 264 ms, with CZ at 284 ms; amplitude maximum at CZ (17.9 µV).  
Session 2 latency minimum at CZ 240 ms; PZ close at 244 ms. Amplitude peaks at CZ (23.4 µV) with marked right-parietal elevation (PZ 18.2 µV, C4 16.2 µV, P4 13.4 µV).  
Session 3 latencies cluster tightly (244–248 ms) across CZ, PZ, C4, P3, P4, with maximum amplitude still at CZ (24.2 µV); right-parietal amplitudes decrease relative to Session 2\.  
These patterns imply a shift from a single-site earliest latency (P4) to a midline-dominant latency (CZ) and then to a distributed near-synchronous CP pattern, while amplitude dominance remains at CZ.  
N100 central-frontal average (yield / µV / ms): | Metric | Session 1 | Session 2 | Session 3 | |---|---|---|---| | Central-frontal N100 | 37 / \-8.8 / 112 | 36 / \-7.7 / 104 | 38 / \-5.1 / 100 |  
Latency shortens by 12 ms across sessions; amplitude becomes less negative, indicating faster but shallower early auditory cortical responses. Yields remain high (≥36).  
Interpretive cautions:  
Delay metric depends on the earliest qualifying CP site; site changes can alter the aggregate without all sites speeding.  
Voltage elevations above target may reflect stronger neural response, state effects, or residual artifact; no low-yield marker is present, but caution is warranted without artifact logs.  
Background EEG Metrics  
State metrics (eyes closed):  
CZ Theta/Beta Power Ratio (target 1.0–2.5): 2.3 ◘ → 1.4 ◘ → 1.5 ◘. Movement from high-normal toward mid-band; low-yield flag persists, limiting confidence.  
F3/F4 Alpha Power Ratio (target 0.9–1.1): 1.0 → 1.0 → 1.0; stable and within target without yield flags.  
Peak frequency (7.0–13.0 Hz, eyes closed): | Region | Session 1 | Session 2 | Session 3 | Target | |---|---|---|---|---| | Frontal | 10.0 Hz | 10.0 Hz | 9.5 Hz | 9.0–11.0 Hz | | Central-Parietal | 10.0 Hz | 10.0 Hz | 9.5 Hz | 9.0–11.0 Hz | | Occipital | 10.0 Hz | 10.0 Hz | 9.5 Hz | 9.0–11.0 Hz |  
Site-level peak alpha frequency (P300 Eyes Closed):  
Sessions 1–2: all sites at 10.0 Hz (F8 is 9.5 in Session 1 per graphic; others 10.0).  
Session 3: all sites 9.5 Hz.  
Frontal/Occipital alpha ratio R (reference 0.8 ± 0.3): 0.56 → 0.64 → 0.51, consistently below the midpoint, indicating relatively stronger occipital than frontal alpha. (Peer feedback correction: reference range is 0.8 ± 0.3, not 0.0 ± 0.3.)  
Magnitude band tables (P300 Eyes Closed, peak-peak µV):  
Posterior alpha is prominent: PZ 36 → 36 → 40; P3 32 → 28 → 37; P4 29 → 31 → 36\.  
Occipital alpha: O1 29 → 30 → 26; O2 28 → 23 → 22\.  
Theta broadly 6–13 µV across sites; beta generally 13–28 µV with modest midline values (e.g., CZ 17 → 20 → 19).  
Trends: Session 2 shows slight increases in alpha/beta at several sites (e.g., C4 alpha 25 vs 18 in Session 1), while Session 3 raises parietal alpha (PZ 40\) but reduces occipital alpha relative to Session 2\.  
Magnitude band tables (Flanker):  
Occipital beta elevated in Sessions 1–2 (O1 56 → 44; O2 60 → 58 µV); Session 3 shows reduction at O1 (33 µV) but persistence at O2 (58 µV).  
Numerous N/A entries in frontal electrodes in Sessions 1–2 indicate yield limitations for Flanker alpha/theta.  
Alpha at parietal sites during Flanker: P3 14 → 12 → 17; P4 14 → 13 → 16; PZ 16 → 15 → 19 (µV).  
Coherence (P300 Eyes Closed):  
Coherence matrices show moderate alpha/theta coupling across midline and interhemispheric pairs; beta coherence is lower.  
Examples from Session 1: FP1–FP2 alpha 0.71; C3–C4 alpha 0.50; P3–P4 alpha 0.45; F3–FZ alpha 0.90.  
Session 2 changes are mixed: FP1–FP2 alpha 0.76; P3–P4 alpha 0.43; CZ–PZ alpha 0.55.  
Session 3: FP1–FP2 alpha 0.78; P3–P4 alpha 0.46; CZ–PZ alpha 0.56. Percent-change plots (pages 5–6) show both increases and decreases relative to Session 1; no monotonic trend is evident.  
Coherence (Flanker):  
Session 1 examples: FP1–FP2 alpha 0.45; C3–C4 alpha 0.38; P3–P4 alpha 0.39.  
Session 2 examples: FP1–FP2 alpha 0.48; C3–C4 alpha 0.24; P3–P4 alpha 0.33; many FP sites N/A.  
Session 3 examples: FP1–FP2 alpha 0.78; C3–C4 alpha 0.45; P3–P4 alpha 0.32; numerous FP and frontal connections N/A. Percent-change visuals again show mixed directions.  
Interpretive boundaries:  
Alpha slowing to 9.5 Hz in Session 3 remains within target; clinical significance is uncertain without symptoms.  
Theta/beta improvements are promising numerically but constrained by low-yield flags.  
Occipital beta elevations in Flanker Sessions 1–2 could reflect task engagement or muscle artifact; without artifact markers, interpretation remains cautious.  
Speculative Commentary and Interpretive Hypotheses  
(Interpretive; not diagnostic or causal. Multiple competing possibilities are preserved.)  
Generator synchronization shift: The earliest qualifying P300 latency shifts from P4 (264 ms) in Session 1 to CZ (240 ms) in Session 2, then to a near-synchronous 244–248 ms cluster across CP sites in Session 3\. This may indicate improved temporal coordination of bilateral parietal generators rather than isolated speed-up of a single source.  
Transient right-parietal recruitment: Session 2 shows heightened right-parietal amplitudes (PZ 18.2 µV, C4 16.2 µV, P4 13.4 µV) alongside the fastest latency and best behavioral scores. Session 3 retains latency gains but right-parietal amplitudes recede, suggesting transient compensatory recruitment that later balances.  
Stage trade-off (N100 vs P300): N100 becomes faster but less negative, while P300 grows in amplitude. This pattern could reflect a shift of processing load from early sensory encoding to later evaluative stages, consistent with “stage opposition” noted in peer review.  
State vulnerability vs metric resilience: Reaction time regresses in Session 3 while P300 latency stays improved. If state factors (sleep/meal) degraded Session 3 performance, P300 latency may be more resilient to mild state changes than RT, whereas amplitude (above target) could be more state-sensitive. Without state logs, this remains speculative.  
Network dominance and ratio constraints: The frontal/occipital alpha ratio (R=0.56→0.64→0.51; ref 0.8±0.3) stays on the low side, implying persistent posterior dominance. This could constrain frontal engagement, requiring parietal regions to sustain P300 amplitude, especially in Session 2\.  
Practice vs validity: TMT-B improves dramatically from Session 1 and stays stable, while TMT-A and RT regress in Session 3\. This pattern might reflect practice effects specific to TMT-B or differences in task engagement; it does not support the earlier (incorrect) notion that TMT-B became faster than TMT-A.  
Coherence stability vs fluctuation: Alpha coherence remains moderate across sessions; percent-change maps show bidirectional shifts, suggesting state-dependent variability rather than structural connectivity change. Beta coherence remains low, indicating limited high-frequency coupling in both rest-like (P300 EC) and task (Flanker) conditions.  
Amplitude above reference: Sessions 2–3 P300 voltages exceed the 22 µV upper target. This may represent heightened cortical responsiveness or unremoved artifact. Without “low yield” markers on P300, the data were acceptable to the system, but interpret amplitude elevations with caution.

scenes.title: Title Card
scenes.narration: What does it look like when a young brain gets sharper? Today we're following the journey of a 27-year-old who received LUMIT treatment over six weeks, and the brain scan data reveals something fascinating. We're going to track three snapshots in time, watching specific brain metrics shift and change. Some improvements were dramatic. Others were more subtle. And one pattern in the final session created a real puzzle we'll need to solve. So let's dive into the data and see what this brain can teach us about recovery and adaptation.
scenes.visual_prompt: Clean, modern title card on dark gradient background (deep navy to black). Large centered text reading "A Young Brain Sharpens" in elegant sans-serif font (like Montserrat). Smaller subtitle below reading "Following Six Weeks of Measurable Change". Subtle abstract brain wave pattern in background with soft blue glow. Small text at bottom reading "Based on qEEG Data - August to September 2024". Premium, cinematic feel with gentle particle effects.

scenes.title: The Roadmap
scenes.narration: Here's what we'll explore together. First, the dramatic breakthrough in executive function. Second, the brain's electrical timing improvements. Third, a puzzling regression in the final session. Fourth, how different brain systems adapted in different ways. And finally, what all of this means for continuing treatment.
scenes.visual_prompt: Dark gradient background with clean white text. Title at top reading "What We'll Discover". Numbered list below in modern sans-serif font: "1. The Executive Function Breakthrough", "2. Faster Brain Processing", "3. The Session 3 Puzzle", "4. How Systems Adapted Differently", "5. What Comes Next". Each item on its own line, left-aligned. Subtle connecting dotted line running down the left side. Modern minimal infographic style with soft blue accent color.

scenes.title: Meet the Patient
scenes.narration: Our patient is a 27-year-old professional. He went through three brain scan sessions: the first on August 5th established his baseline, the second on August 26th checked early progress, and the final session on September 20th assessed where things stood after six weeks. The scans measured everything from reaction speed to complex problem-solving to the brain's electrical patterns.
scenes.visual_prompt: Clean timeline visualization on dark background. Three milestone markers connected by horizontal line. Left marker labeled "Session 1" with text "August 5" and subtitle "Baseline". Middle marker labeled "Session 2" with text "August 26" and subtitle "Progress Check". Right marker labeled "Session 3" with text "September 20" and subtitle "Final Assessment". Small text at top reading "27-Year-Old Male - Treatment Timeline". Modern infographic style with soft blue glowing markers.

scenes.title: The Measurement Tool
scenes.narration: The technology used is called quantitative EEG, or qEEG for short. Think of it as a detailed performance report for the brain. It measures processing speed, reaction time, problem-solving ability, and the brain's electrical wave patterns. Every number we'll discuss comes from these precise, objective measurements taken across all three sessions.
scenes.visual_prompt: Stylized illustration of brain scan setup. Central image: simple 3D head profile with subtle electrode cap visible. Text labels pointing to different aspects: "Processing Speed" pointing to temporal area, "Wave Patterns" pointing to top of head, "Reaction Time" pointing to frontal area, "Problem Solving" pointing to parietal area. Title at top reading "Quantitative Brain Mapping". Subtitle at bottom reading "Objective, Data-Driven Measurements". Soft medical illustration style with gentle blue-purple color scheme.

scenes.title: The Breakthrough
scenes.narration: Let's start with the most dramatic finding. There's a test called Trail Making Test B that measures executive function—your ability to juggle multiple mental tasks, switch attention, and solve complex problems. In the first session, it took him 105 seconds. By session two, just 57 seconds. Session three held steady at 58 seconds. That's nearly cutting the time in half. This is a massive improvement in cognitive flexibility and mental agility.
scenes.visual_prompt: Split comparison graphic. Left side: stopwatch showing "105" in large numbers, label "Session 1" below, subtitle "August 5". Right side: stopwatch showing "57" in large numbers, label "Sessions 2 & 3" below, subtitle "Aug 26 / Sep 20". Large arrow pointing from left to right with text "-46%" on the arrow. Top banner text reading "Executive Function Breakthrough". Bottom text reading "Trail Making Test B (seconds)". Bright, celebratory color scheme with gold accent on the improved time.

scenes.title: Session 2 Peak
scenes.narration: Here's what makes session two so interesting. It wasn't just the executive function test. His reaction time hit 238 milliseconds, faster than the target range. The simpler Trail Making Test A dropped to 36 seconds. And his brain's electrical response, the P300 signal, clocked in at 240 milliseconds—the fastest of all three sessions. Session two was peak performance across the board.
scenes.visual_prompt: Dashboard-style graphic showing three metrics side-by-side. First panel: circular gauge with needle in green zone, text "238 ms" below, label "Reaction Time". Second panel: similar gauge, text "36 sec" below, label "Trail Making A". Third panel: similar gauge, text "240 ms" below, label "P300 Speed". Top banner reading "Session 2: Peak Performance". Subtitle "August 26, 2024". Bottom text "All Metrics in Optimal Range". Modern medical dashboard aesthetic with glowing green accents.

scenes.title: Brain Timing Synchronization
scenes.narration: Now let's look at what changed in the brain itself. The P300 is an electrical signal that shows how fast the brain processes information. In session one, different brain regions fired at different times—the earliest response came at 264 milliseconds. By session two, the central region sped up to 240 milliseconds. But here's what's really fascinating: by session three, six different brain regions all synchronized to fire at nearly the same moment, clustered between 244 and 248 milliseconds. The brain wasn't just getting faster—it was getting more coordinated.
scenes.visual_prompt: {previous scene: Dashboard-style graphic showing three metrics side-by-side. First panel: circular gauge with needle in green zone, text "238 ms" below, label "Reaction Time". Second panel: similar gauge, text "36 sec" below, label "Trail Making A". Third panel: similar gauge, text "240 ms" below, label "P300 Speed". Top banner reading "Session 2: Peak Performance". Subtitle "August 26, 2024". Bottom text "All Metrics in Optimal Range". Modern medical dashboard aesthetic with glowing green accents.} Now showing brain synchronization change. Abstract top-down view of brain with six glowing points representing electrode sites (C3, CZ, C4, P3, PZ, P4). Left image labeled "Session 1": rightmost point flashes first at "264 ms", other points dim and unsynchronized with text "Scattered Timing". Right image labeled "Session 3": all six points flash simultaneously in beautiful unified burst, text "244-248 ms" centered below with label "Synchronized". Connecting arrow between images. Title at top reading "From Scattered to Synchronized". Soft neural network aesthetic, blue-purple tones.

scenes.title: Voltage Amplification
scenes.narration: The P300 signal also got stronger. Think of voltage like the brain's signal strength or volume. The target range is 9 to 22 microvolts. Session one measured 17.9 microvolts, right in the middle of the range. By sessions two and three, it jumped to 23.4 and then 24.2 microvolts—exceeding the upper target. This suggests the brain was recruiting more neural resources, putting more power behind its information processing.
scenes.visual_prompt: Three vertical bar meters side-by-side, like audio level meters. Each meter shows a scale from 9 to 26 microvolts with target zone (9-22) marked in green, above-target zone in gold. Left meter: filled to 17.9 µV, label "Session 1" below, subtitle "Aug 5". Middle meter: filled to 23.4 µV (into gold zone), label "Session 2" below, subtitle "Aug 26". Right meter: filled to 24.2 µV (into gold zone), label "Session 3" below, subtitle "Sep 20". Title at top reading "Signal Strength Amplification". Bottom text "P300 Voltage (microvolts)". Modern data visualization style.

scenes.title: The Session 3 Puzzle
scenes.narration: But here's where things get interesting. While the brain's P300 timing stayed improved in session three and executive function remained dramatically better, reaction time actually regressed. It went from 238 milliseconds in session two back up to 287 milliseconds in session three—even slower than the baseline. Why would some improvements stick while others backslid? This is the puzzle we need to solve.
scenes.visual_prompt: Line graph showing reaction time trend across three sessions. X-axis labeled with three points: "Aug 5" "Aug 26" "Sep 20". Y-axis showing milliseconds from 200 to 300. Line starts at 253 ms (Session 1, marked with blue dot), drops to 238 ms (Session 2, green dot and star icon), then rises sharply to 287 ms (Session 3, red dot with question mark icon). Title at top reading "The Regression Mystery". Subtitle "Why Did Reaction Time Slow Down?". Bottom label "Physical Reaction Time (milliseconds)". Clean graph style with contrasting colors to show the unexpected reversal.

scenes.title: Metric Resilience
scenes.narration: The answer likely lies in what we might call metric resilience. Different brain measurements are vulnerable to different things. Reaction time is sensitive to moment-to-moment factors like sleep quality, caffeine intake, even what time of day you take the test. The P300 electrical signal and executive function tests appear to be more resilient—they measure something more fundamental that isn't as easily disrupted by a bad day. Session three's reaction time might simply reflect that he wasn't at his best that morning.
scenes.visual_prompt: Two-column comparison. Left column header "Sensitive to Daily State" with icon of person looking tired. Below: three items listed: text "Reaction Time", "Trail Making A", "Energy Levels". Right column header "Resilient Core Metrics" with icon of strong pillar/foundation. Below: three items: text "P300 Latency", "Executive Function", "Brain Synchronization". Center dividing line. Title at top reading "Why Some Metrics Changed Differently". Bottom text "Understanding Measurement Stability". Clean infographic style with contrasting colors (left side warm amber, right side cool blue).

scenes.title: Processing Stage Shift
scenes.narration: There's another fascinating pattern in the data. The N100 signal, which represents early sensory processing, got faster but weaker. Meanwhile, the P300 signal, which represents higher-level thinking, got stronger. This suggests the brain was shifting its processing strategy—relying less on rapid early reactions and more on thoughtful, deliberate processing. It's working smarter, not just faster.
scenes.visual_prompt: Side-by-side brain processing diagrams. Left diagram labeled "Early Processing (N100)" shows lightning bolt symbol at top of brain, arrow pointing down labeled "Getting Weaker", text below "-8.8 → -5.1 microvolts" and "Faster but Shallower". Right diagram labeled "Higher Processing (P300)" shows glowing orb in center of brain, arrow pointing up labeled "Getting Stronger", text below "17.9 → 24.2 microvolts" and "More Power, More Depth". Title at top reading "Strategic Processing Shift". Subtitle "From Reactive to Thoughtful". Soft illustration style with color contrast (cool blue for N100, warm gold for P300).

scenes.title: Attention Regulation
scenes.narration: The theta/beta ratio is a key marker for attention and mental regulation. Higher ratios can indicate distractibility or mental fog. His ratio started at 2.3, which was at the high end of the normal range. By session two, it dropped to 1.4, and session three held at 1.5. This movement toward the mid-range suggests improved attention regulation and reduced mental noise.
scenes.visual_prompt: Horizontal slider graphic showing theta/beta ratio scale from 1.0 to 2.5. Target range marked with green zone (1.0-2.5), with optimal zone highlighted in darker green (1.0-1.8). Three position markers: First marker at 2.3 labeled "Session 1" and "High-Normal". Second marker at 1.4 labeled "Session 2". Third marker at 1.5 labeled "Session 3" and "Optimal Range". Left side of scale labeled "Better Focus", right side labeled "More Distractibility". Title at top reading "Attention Regulation Improvement". Bottom text "Theta/Beta Power Ratio". Clean gauge design with green accent color.

scenes.title: Alpha Frequency Slowing
scenes.narration: Peak alpha frequency, which reflects the brain's basic operating rhythm, slowed slightly from 10.0 to 9.5 Hertz across all brain regions. This remained well within the normal range of 9.0 to 11.0 Hertz. A minor slowing like this can be part of the brain settling into a more relaxed, efficient baseline rather than operating in a high-alert state.
scenes.visual_prompt: Two brain outlines side-by-side showing wave patterns. Left brain labeled "Sessions 1 & 2" with faster wave oscillations overlaid, text "10.0 Hz" below. Right brain labeled "Session 3" with slightly slower wave oscillations, text "9.5 Hz" below. Both waves colored in soft blue. Green horizontal band spanning both images labeled "Normal Range: 9.0-11.0 Hz". Title at top reading "Alpha Rhythm Adjustment". Bottom text "Still Well Within Healthy Range". Soft neuroscience illustration style with emphasis on waves maintaining normal pattern.

scenes.title: Parietal Recruitment Pattern
scenes.narration: One more intriguing detail. In session two, the right parietal region of the brain showed unusually high activity—like the brain was calling in extra reinforcements to support the improvements. By session three, that heightened activity had settled back down, but the core improvements in timing and executive function remained. This suggests the brain used temporary compensation to establish new patterns, then relaxed once those patterns were stable.
scenes.visual_prompt: Three brain side-view diagrams showing right parietal region. First brain labeled "Session 1" with dim glow in parietal area, text "Baseline Activity". Second brain labeled "Session 2" with very bright glowing parietal region, text "High Recruitment" and small sparkle effects. Third brain labeled "Session 3" with moderate glow in parietal area (brighter than Session 1, dimmer than Session 2), text "Stable Integration". Arrow flowing left to right connecting the three. Title at top reading "Temporary Reinforcement Strategy". Bottom text "Right Parietal Region". Soft medical visualization style with glowing effects in blue-purple-gold gradient.

scenes.title: Pattern Synthesis
scenes.narration: So let's put all the pieces together. The dramatic improvement in executive function—cutting problem-solving time nearly in half—is the headline finding. The brain's electrical signals became faster and more synchronized. Attention regulation improved. The processing strategy shifted from reactive to thoughtful. Yes, reaction time regressed in the final session, but that appears to be day-to-day variability rather than loss of core gains. The fundamental cognitive improvements held strong.
scenes.visual_prompt: Central hub diagram with six connected nodes. Center circle contains brain icon with text "Core Transformation". Six spokes radiating outward to circular nodes: Node 1: "Executive Function +46%" with checkmark. Node 2: "Faster Processing" with checkmark. Node 3: "Better Synchronization" with checkmark. Node 4: "Improved Focus" with checkmark. Node 5: "Strategic Shift" with checkmark. Node 6: "Reaction Time" with question mark (different color, amber vs green for others). Connecting lines between center and nodes. Title at top reading "The Overall Pattern". Bottom text "Fundamental Gains Established". Modern network diagram style with clean typography.

scenes.title: Conclusion and Next Steps
scenes.narration: This patient's journey shows us something important about brain adaptation. After six weeks of LUMIT treatment, the data reveals meaningful, measurable changes. The brain became faster at processing information, more synchronized in its activity, and dramatically more efficient at complex problem-solving. While some metrics showed day-to-day fluctuation, the core improvements in brain function are clearly established. Based on this strong response, continuing LUMIT treatment is likely to consolidate these gains even further and potentially unlock additional improvements. The foundation for lasting change has been built.
scenes.visual_prompt: Clean conclusion slide on dark gradient background. Top third: Text reading "The Evidence Is Clear" in large elegant font. Middle section shows three key metrics in clean boxes: Box 1: "Executive Function" with text "105s → 57s" and green upward arrow. Box 2: "Brain Synchronization" with text "Scattered → Unified" and green checkmark. Box 3: "Processing Speed" with text "264ms → 244ms" and green upward arrow. Bottom third: Text reading "Recommendation: Continue LUMIT Treatment" in bold, followed by smaller text "Strong Response Supports Ongoing Therapy". Professional medical report aesthetic with blue-gold accent colors.
